title: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome (5 Volumes)
paragraphs: The struggle of parties during Pompeius VI's absence was a necessary step in explaining alphabetic development. A single symbol could represent multiple sounds across various languages or even within the same language over time. Thirty-three years had passed since such an introduction was first introduced, yet I still cherish how much the book owed its initial success to Dr. Schmitz's friendly assistance.

I did not take on this task until I had investigated the possibility of someone else being better suited to handle it. When Dr Mommsen's work somehow came into my hands years after its initial appearance, and it revived my interest in studies I had long set aside for more professional pursuits. I had little doubt that its merits would have already attracted sufficient attention among Oxford's learned community, inducing some of her great scholars to translate it into English. It seemed that despite a strong desire to translate it, and several individuals having considered the task, the projects ultimately failed due to various reasons. Mr George Robertson published an excellent translation of the introductory chapters on the early inhabitants of Italy. I wish to acknowledge my obligations to this work, as far as it goes.

Many of our translations from German are so literal that they reproduce the very order of the German sentence. This results in translations that are either unintelligible or far from readable for the English reader. In contrast, others deviate so entirely from the original form that they cease to be true translations in the proper sense of the term. This, however, is a characteristic feature of the original. It owes much of its charm to this aspect. Dr Mommsen often employs expressions not found in dictionaries, taking full advantage of the German language's limitless possibilities for coining new words or combining existing ones. I have not often, in respect of his wishes, employed combinations such as "Carthaginian" and "Hellenistic," though less familiar to our English style, to avoid longer phrases. "In Dr As with Mommsen's book, many other German works that deal with abstract topics often employ unusual terminology, making them difficult to translate.

The Index, which had not been issued for the German book until nine years after the English translation was published, has now been greatly enlarged from its earlier German form, and has been, at the expense of considerable labor, adapted to the altered pagination of the English. The cycle that includes cities such as Thebes, Carthage, Athens, and Rome can be considered a single entity. The four nations, having each achieved a unique and noble civilization on their own paths, interacted with one another in diverse ways, skillfully combining and enriching the various aspects of human nature.

The distinction between ancient and modern history is not merely an accident, nor simply a matter of chronological convenience. The Apennines take a south-eastern course initially, between the broader gulf of the Mediterranean to the west and the narrower one to the east. In the vicinity of the latter, they reach their greatest elevation, which, however, rarely reaches the line of perpetual snow, in the Abruzzi Mountains. The mountain range continues from the Abruzzi in a southern direction, at first undivided and of considerable height. After a depression that forms a hill country, it splits into two sections: one becomes a somewhat flattened succession of heights towards the south-east, while the other remains more rugged and terminates towards the south. In both directions, the range eventually forms narrow peninsulas.

The Greek peninsula faces east, while the Italian peninsula faces west. As the coasts of Epirus and Acarnania had little importance in Hellas, so did the Apulian and Messapian coasts in Italy. The regions that were mainly dependent on historical development in Greece—Attica and Macedonia—looked eastward, while Etruria, Latium, and Campania looked westward. Although the naked eye can discern from Otranto the Acroceraunian mountains, the Italians and Hellenes came into earlier and closer contact on every other pathway rather than on the nearest route across the Adriatic Sea. In their instance, as has often happened, the historical vocation of the nations was prefigured in the relations of the ground they occupied. The two great stocks, on which the civilization of the ancient world grew, cast a shadow and sowed a seed, one towards the east and the other towards the west.

It was the civic community of Rome that initially gained sovereignty over Italy and eventually the world, in the formal sense of political law. However, this perspective does not accurately convey the deeper, more profound meaning of history. U C  C Apulia, which in the time of Timaeus (400) was still described as a barbarous land, became a Greek province by the sixth century of its founding. Despite no direct colonization from Greece having occurred, the region showed signs of a similar cultural inclination among the Messapii, a less refined subgroup.

With the recognition of such a general family relationship or peculiar affinity between the Iapygians and Hellenes, an investigation must rest content for the time being, pending the attainment of more precise and assured results. The lack of information is not greatly felt; for this race, which was already in decline during the time our history begins, is only visible when it is giving way and disappearing. The excessive disorder of the terminations in Umbrian was not consistent with its original spirit, but rather a corruption of later date. This phenomenon also occurred in Rome, albeit to a lesser extent. In Italian languages, short vowels are regularly dropped in the final sound, whereas long ones are frequently retained. In contrast, the concluding consonants have been tenaciously retained in Latin and even more so in Samnite dialects. The Umbrian language, however, drops even these consonants.

In connection with this, we find that the middle voice has left but slight traces in Italian languages. A peculiar passive is formed by adding -r, which takes its place. Furthermore, the majority of tenses are formed by composition with roots -es and -fu. In contrast, the Greeks' richer terminational system, combined with the augment, enables them to dispense with auxiliary verbs to a great extent. The Italian languages, like the Aeolic dialect, abandoned the dual form but retained the ablative case, which the Greeks had previously lost, and in large measure also preserved the locative case. The Italians' rigorous logic seems to have taken issue with the separation of the concept of plurality into that of duality and multitude. They have made precise expressions for the relationships between words through inflections.

Some, such as the Martian and Volscian, have reached us in fragments too scanty to form any conception of their individual peculiarities or classify the varieties of dialect with certainty. Others, like the Sabine, have disappeared, except for a few traces preserved in provincial Latin. A conjoint view of the facts of language and history leaves no doubt that all these dialects belonged to the Umbro-Samnite branch of the great Italian stock. This branch, although more closely related to Latin than to Greek, was distinctly separate from the Latin. The genitive forms of words ending in -a are found in this group: among the Greeks, they end in -as, whereas those in the matured Roman language end in -ae. In contrast, words ending in -us have genitive forms in -eis (in Samnite) and -es (in Umbrian), with the Romans using -ei. The locative case has largely fallen out of use in the latter dialects, whereas it remains fully functional in other Italian dialects. Notably, the dative plural ending in -bus is only preserved in Latin. The Umbro-Samnite infinitive in -um is foreign to the Romans. In contrast, the Osco-Umbrian future formed from the root -es, following Greek patterns (-her-estlike), has largely disappeared in Latin. Instead, it is replaced by the optative of the simple verb or analogous formations derived from -fuo- (-amabo-).

In many cases, however, the differences only emerge when the forms are fully developed, whereas at the beginning they overlap. It appears that, while Italian stands apart from Greek, the Latin dialect within it shares a relation with the Umbro-Samnite similar to that of Ionic with Doric; and the differences between Oscan and Umbrian dialects can be compared to the differences between Doric in Sicily and Doric in Sparta. With perfect certainty, they guide us to the conclusion that from the common cradle of peoples and languages, a stock emerged which included the ancestors of both Greeks and Italians. This stock subsequently branched off into two groups: the Italians and those who would eventually become the western and eastern stocks. The latter group then split further, with the eastern stock dividing into the Umbrians and Oscans at a later date.

During the period when the Indo-Germanic nations, which are now separated, still shared a common heritage, they developed a distinct culture. This cultural milestone was marked by a shared vocabulary that reflected their linguistic ties. In it we find not merely the simplest terms denoting existence, actions, and perceptions, such as -sum-, -do-, and -pater-. These words are the original echo of the impression made by the external world on the mind of man. They also include a number of words that reflect culture, both in their origins and through customs that have shaped them. This is true for the Indo-Germanic family, whose members share these words without explanation. Although we have reached no conclusive results through this approach, a new perspective on the subject emerges from our observation that many key terms relevant to this cultural domain are indeed found in Sanskrit, albeit with a broader meaning. Agras among the Indians denote a level surface in general. Kurnu refers to anything pounded. Aritram denotes an oar and a ship. Venas signifies what is pleasant in general, particularly a pleasant drink.

We may accordingly assume it is probable that the primeval Indo-Germanic people were not yet acquainted with agriculture. It is certain that if they were so, agriculture played but a very subordinate part in their economy. If it had held its place among the Greeks and Romans as it did afterwards, it would have left a deeper impression on their language. This cannot, however, be equally affirmed of the higher art of weaving. The knowledge of using fire to prepare food and salt for seasoning is a primeval heritage of the Indo-Germanic nations. Similarly, the knowledge of the earliest metals used as tools or adornments by humans can also be affirmed. The conception of heaven as the father and earth as the mother of being, for example, is a fundamental idea in both Indian and Greek mythology. The gods' festal expeditions, where they travel from place to place in their own chariots along carefully levelled paths, are also an important concept. Additionally, the shadowy continuation of the soul's existence after death is another key idea shared by these mythologies.

The Graeco-Italian cultural influence is a topic that warrants examination. However, the task of determining the extent of culture achieved by the Indo-Germanic peoples prior to their geographical separation falls within the realm of general historical inquiry. The Indo-Germans likely lived a pastoral lifestyle and may have been familiar with grains only in their natural state. However, all available evidence suggests that the Graeco-Italians were primarily grain cultivators, possibly also wine producers. It would thus appear that the transition from a pastoral life to agriculture occurred when the two merged, rather than the other way around. This combination likely took place after the Indians left the shared origin of the nations, but before the Greeks and Italians severed their ancient ties.

For the present, therefore, it must suffice to note the differences between the culture of the Indo-Germanic family in its oldest, undivided form, and the culture of that epoch when the Graeco-Italians still coexisted. The task of distinguishing results common to European family members from those unique to its Asiatic members can only be achieved after significant progress has been made in linguistic and historical research. Among the Italians and the Hellenes, legend and faith, laws and customs, are closely tied to agriculture. Cultivation of the soil cannot be conceived without some measurement of it, however rough.

The Greeks and Italians have a particularly close relationship beyond agriculture, which is evident in other areas of human activity. The boat with oars was an old common possession of the Indo-Germans. However, the transition to using sailing vessels cannot be attributed to the Graeco-Italian period, as no nautical terms originally shared by the Greeks and Italians were found among the Indo-Germanic family.

On the other hand, the primitive Italian custom of husbandmen having common midday meals is connected to the myth of its origin, which dates back to the introduction of agriculture. Aristotle compares this practice with the Cretan Syssitia. The earliest Romans also adopted this tradition, agreeing with the Cretans and Laconians in taking their meals while sitting, rather than reclining as was later customary among both peoples. While under Greece's weaker political development, the clan-bond remained a corporate power distinct from that of the state, even into historical times. In contrast, the state in Italy emerged complete, with its authority neutralizing the clans and forming an association of citizens rather than clans. In ancient Greece, a clan's name was often combined with an adjective form of its individual member's name. In contrast, Roman scholars recognized that their ancestors initially had only one name, known as praenomen. While in Greece, the clan-name as an adjective had largely fallen out of use, it became a primary name among the Italians, not just the Romans. The distinctive personal name, known as praenomen, then took on secondary importance.

It could not be otherwise; for just as in the peoples themselves, great contrasts that had lain side by side during the Graeco-Italian period were distinctly evolved after their division, so too did a separation occur between the idea and the image in their religion. Hitherto, these two concepts had been one whole within the soul. To everything existing, from the man to the tree, from the state to the store-room, was assigned a spirit that came into being with it and perished along with it. This counterpart mirrored the natural phenomenon in the spiritual realm; for humans, this spirit took the form of the male Genius, while women were associated with the female Juno. Similarly, boundaries were represented by Terminus, forests by Silvanus, and the cyclical nature of time was embodied by Vertumnus. While the Greeks assumed everything had a concrete and corporeal shape, the Romans relied on abstract, transparent formulas. In contrast, the Greeks largely discarded their ancient, legendary treasures from primitive times, as they were too straightforward in conveying their ideas. The Romans, however, found it even more difficult to retain these treasures, as the sacred conceptions seemed diminished by even the slightest hint of allegory.

The training of youth was strictly confined to domestic education in Latium. In contrast, ancient Greece fostered a desire for a well-rounded training that balanced the development of mind and body. This led to the emergence of two distinct disciplines: Gymnastics and Paideia. Both were highly valued by the nation and individuals alike, representing their highest aspirations for personal growth and excellence. Both nations underwent complete development on one side; it is only a pitiful narrow-mindedness that will object to the Athenian that he did not know how to shape his state like the Fabii and the Valerii, or to the Roman that he did not learn to carve like Pheidias and write like Aristophanes.

The connection between agriculture and marriage was a crucial aspect of early cultures. The establishment of cities also played a vital role during this period. The lance was one of the oldest names of weapons on both sides. Few can be shown to be certainly related. Lancea, although likely connected to logcheia, is a Roman word that appears to be relatively recent. It may have been borrowed from the Germans or Spaniards. 9 The origin of the Indo-Germanic people remains unclear. However, it is likely that their ancestral homeland was located inland, far from the coast. This is because there is no shared term for a sea that would be applicable to both the Asian and European branches of the Indo-Germanic languages.

This circumstance suggests that these races may have had an original connection, which would imply that any such link existed before the observable development of culture and language. The name for the sea is shared by many European countries, including Latins, Celts, Germans, and Slavs. It's likely that these groups converged on the coast of either the Black Sea or the Caspian Sea before their separation. By what route did the Italians reach the chain of the Alps, and where were they specifically settled while still united with the Hellenes alone? These questions can only be answered once the problem is solved by determining whether the Hellenes arrived in Greece via Asia Minor or the regions of the Danube.

The stream only overflows when the lower grounds are already occupied. It is only through the supposition that there were existing Latin settlements on the coast that we can explain why the Sabellians chose to settle in the rougher mountain districts. From these districts, they later issued and intruded into the territories of the Latin tribes wherever possible. The primitive inhabitants of the districts occupied by the Lucani and Bruttii, who are known as the Itali proper (the people of cattle), are associated with the Italian stock by the most reliable observers. They are not linked to the Iapygian but rather to the Italian branch of the Latin people. This connection is supported because the Hellenization of these districts, which occurred even before Italy's political development began, and their subsequent invasion by Samnite tribes, have erased all evidence of their original nationality. The affinity of certain dialectical features in Sicilian Greek with Latin is likely due to the historical trade links between Rome and the Sicilians, rather than their shared linguistic heritage.

It was otherwise with Latium, where no Greek colonies were founded. The inhabitants, having endured hard struggles, successfully maintained their territory against both the Sabines and their northern neighbors. The plain of Latium must have been the scene of the grandest conflicts of nature in primeval times. The slowly forming agency of water deposited, and the eruptions of mighty volcanoes, upheaved the successive strata of that soil. It was on this soil that the question of which people should claim sovereignty over the world would be decided. It is true, however, that malaria can be largely eradicated through thorough farming practices—a fact that remains to be fully explained, but may be partly attributed to the accelerated drying of stagnant waters caused by surface cultivation.

In other respects, the district must have appeared attractive to an immigrant agricultural people. The soil is easily laboured with a mattock and hoe and is productive even without being manured. Although tried by an Italian standard, it does not yield any extraordinary return. Wheat yields on average about five times its value. Good water is not abundant. The higher and more sacred esteem for it was held by the inhabitants of every fresh spring. Some knowledge may, however, be gained in this way, or at least some conjectures that are based on probability. As each household had its own portion of land, so too did the clan-household or village have a clan-land belonging to it. This arrangement, as will be explained later, was managed in a manner similar to household-land, following the principle of joint-possession.

Cantons. These clanships, however, were regarded from the beginning not as independent societies, but as integral parts of a political community (civitas, populus). In Italy, this place was known as either "height" (capitolium) or "stronghold" (arx), derived from the Latin word "arcere". Initially, it wasn't a town, but rather a central point that attracted houses, which eventually formed a settlement around it. The stronghold was then surrounded by a "ring" (urbs), possibly connected to the concepts of "urvus", "rurvus", and "orbis". We shall certainly be nearer the truth in recognizing these structures not as walled towns, but as places of refuge for the inhabitants of the district, such as those found in more ancient times throughout Italy, although built in a less artistic style.

When peace was securely established throughout the land, these places of refuge were abandoned. During the Latin festival, a truce of God was observed throughout all Latium, as it had been during the festivals of the Hellenic leagues. Tribes at feud likely granted safe-conducts to one another on this occasion. Like -latus- (side) and —platus— (flat), it denotes a flat country in contrast to the Sabine mountain land. Similarly, Campania, the "plain", forms a contrast with Samnium.

Sat i iv l c ) 6 All history begins not with the union, but with the disunion of a nation. It is highly unlikely that the problem of uniting Latium, which Rome eventually resolved after several centuries of conflict, had already been solved at an earlier time by Alba. After the union, each of these three communities – once separate but now forming subdivisions of a single community – still held one-third of the common domain and enjoyed proportional representation in both the burgess force and the council of the elders.

Leaving aside self-contradictory and unfounded hypotheses, we can summarize in a few words what can be said about the nationality of the component elements of the primitive Roman commonwealth. The Romans were indeed a Latin people, and it is clear that they played a significant role in shaping the identity of the new Roman Commonwealth. Their influence on the community's nationality cannot be overstated, as their name became synonymous with the new entity. It may be that at a period very remote, when the Latin and Sabellian stocks were less sharply contrasted in language, manners, and customs than they were later, a Sabellian community entered into a Latin confederation; and, as in older traditions without exception, the Tities took precedence of the Ramnians. It is probable that the intruding Tities compelled the older Ramnians to accept a form of synoikism.

A mixture of different nationalities certainly took place. However, this did not have a significant impact compared to the migration that occurred later, such as when the Sabine Attus Clauzus or Appius Claudius and his followers arrived in Rome. The city of Rome was likely established before a major urban settlement developed along the Tiber River. The region's original inhabitants, including the Ramnians, Tities, and Luceres, initially lived separately but eventually united under a single leadership. They established their stronghold on the Roman hills and cultivated their fields using resources from surrounding villages. Neither the vine nor the fig succeed well in the immediate environs. There is a lack of springs yielding a good supply of water. The Camenae fountain before the Porta Capena and the Capitoline well, later enclosed within the Tullianum, do not provide sufficient water.

Its very slight fall renders it unable to carry off the water, which during the rainy season descends in large quantities from the mountains to the sea. As a result, the low-lying lands and valleys that lie between the hills become flooded, transforming them into swamps. It is the first duty of history to dismiss tales that claim to be historical but are merely improvised explanations of little significance. However, it may be permissible to take a step further and, after considering the special connections between the location, propose a positive conjecture. This should focus on the circumstances that led to the area's rapid growth and its unique position in Latium, rather than the origin of the place itself. In contrast, while the Roman canton was limited to a landward direction, it stretched freely along both banks of the Tiber towards the sea.

The galley arrived in the city; this also brought ancient Roman port duties on exports and imports of Ostia, which were first levied only on goods intended for sale, not on those reserved for the shipper's use. As a result, these duties constituted a tax on commerce. Giorgio in Velabro, and the Porta Mugionis at the Arch of Titus, are still known to us. The Palatine ring-wall is described by Tacitus from his own observation, particularly on its sides facing the Aventine and Caelian hills. The public assemblies of the community were early transferred to another locality, so that their original site is unknown. It may be conjectured that the free space around the mundus, later known as the area Apollinis, was the primary place of assembly for the burgesses and the senate. The stage erected over the mundus itself served as the primitive seat of justice for the Roman community.

The suburbs developed in succession, each shielded by its own distinct but less robust enclosure and connected to the original Palatine wall, just like fen districts where the outer dikes merge with the main dike. The additions, manifestly the result of a gradual growth, clearly reveal to a certain extent the earliest history of Palatine Rome, particularly when compared with the Servian arrangement of districts, which was later formed on the basis of this earliest division. The urban settlement, however, began at Rome as well as elsewhere, but not within, rather under the protection of, the stronghold. The oldest settlements with which we are acquainted, and those that afterwards formed the first and second regions in the Servian division of the city, lay in a circle around the Palatine. Pietro in Vincoli, located below the valley, seems to be more significant and possibly older than the settlements established under the Servian arrangement in the Palatine district.

Each of the three tribes - Ramnes, Tities, and Luceres - must have been distributed throughout both the oldest city's two regions: the Subura and Palatine, as well as its suburban region. This distribution may be connected to the fact that, in addition to the Subura and Palatine, each of the subsequent regions added to the city also had three pairs of Argean chapels. As the leaves of the forest make way for the new growth of spring, though they fall unnoticed by human eyes, so too has this unknown city of the Seven Mountains made room for the Rome of history. The Hill-Roman city on the Quirinal was not the only one that existed in ancient times within the circle of the Servian walls. Opposite to it, in its immediate vicinity, lay another city also on the Quirinal. Thus, too, we discover an explanation for the reason why the Subura was built beyond the city wall in the valley between the Esquiline and Quirinal. It was at this point that the two territories met, and the Palatine Romans, after taking possession of the low ground, were compelled to construct a stronghold for protection against those on the Quirinal. As the Palatine city took the name of "the Seven Mounts," its citizens referred to themselves as the "mount-men" (-montani-). The term "mount" was applied to the other heights within the city, with the Palatine being particularly associated with it. Consequently, the Quirinal height, although not lower but somewhat higher than the former, and the adjacent Viminal, were never referred to by any name other than "hill" (collis) in strict usage of the language.

We have already discussed how the Subura and Palatine annually contested for the horse's head; several Mounts also, and even several curies (there was no common hearth for the city, but the various hearths of the curies coexisted side by side) likely felt more separated than united. Rome as a whole was probably an aggregate of urban settlements rather than a single city. It appears from various indications that the houses of the old and powerful families were built in a style similar to fortresses and were designed to be defensible—a prudent measure, likely deemed necessary. The synoikismos did not necessarily involve an actual settlement together at one spot. Instead, each person continued to reside on their own land. However, from that point forward, there was only one council hall and courthouse for the entire community. ii Herodotus i 170) 3

Although the older opinion that Latin is to be viewed as a mixed language composed of Greek and non-Greek elements has largely been abandoned, judicious inquirers still maintain that it was so. g Research The form commonly occurring in authors, such as Lupercus Quinctilius and Quinctilianus, is actually a misnomer. The college belonged to the far older Quinctii family, rather than the comparatively recent Quinctii. Although the name "Hill of Quirinus" was afterwards ordinarily used to designate the height where the Hill-Romans had their abode, we need not consider the name "Quirites" as originally reserved solely for the burgesses on the Quirinal. For as has been shown, all the earliest indications point to the name -Collini-, while it is indisputably certain that the name Quirites denoted from the first, as well as subsequently, simply the full burgess. chap v infra)

The later designation of the Quirinal is based on the fact that, while the spear-bearing god of Death, Mars quirinus, was originally worshipped on both the Palatine and the Quirinal, as evidenced by the oldest inscriptions found at what would later be known as the Temple of Quirinus, which simply referred to this deity as Mars. Over time, for the sake of distinction, the god specifically associated with the Mount-Romans was referred to as Mars, while the god revered by the Hill Romans was called Quirinus. The Roman gods Mars Quirinus, Sun, Salus, Flora, and Semo Sancus (or Deus Fidius) were undoubtedly Sabine, but they were also Latin deities, formed clearly during the time when both Latins and Sabines coexisted. If a name such as Semo Sancus, which also appears in connection with the Tiber Island, is particularly associated with the sacred sites on the Quirinal, which later lost its significance. The Porta Sanqualis derives its name from there. Every unbiased inquirer will recognize in such a circumstance only a proof of the high antiquity of that worship, not a proof of its derivation from a neighboring land.

Children born to daughters were excluded. If born in wedlock, they belonged to their father's family. If born out of wedlock, they had no place in a family. The death of an individual was not evil, for it was a matter of necessity. However, the extinction of a household or clan was injurious to the community itself. In the earliest times, this led the community to open up to childless individuals the means of avoiding such a fatality by adopting the children of others as their own. Woman was not a servant within the house; she was its mistress.

The family was governed by the single, all-powerful will of the "father of the household," or pater familias. The only instance of support accorded by the community in Rome is the enactment that aid should be given to the father who had three children presented to him at birth. Their ideas regarding exposure are indicated by the prohibition of it, so far as concerned all sons—deformed births excepted—and at least the first daughter. The prohibition of exposure soon changed its form from a legal punishment to a religious curse. The father, being the absolute master of his household, made it so.

As long as the father lived, his children could never own property themselves, and thus could not transfer it without his permission, except through a will. A father might transfer property to both his son and his slave to a third party. If the buyer was a foreigner, the son became a slave. If the buyer was a Roman, the son could not become a Roman's slave, but he stood at least in the same position as a slave (-in mancipii causa-) towards his purchaser. The power of the master of the household over his wife and child closely approximated to his proprietary power over slaves and cattle. However, this did not create a uniform separation between the family members and their property. Rather, there existed a broad line of distinction, both in fact and law, that separated the family from its assets. Their rights were dormant in respect of exercise because the unity of the household demanded it be governed by a single representative. However, when the master of the household died, his sons immediately took control as its masters and now obtained the rights they had previously exercised over women and children and property on their own account.

Both denoted the male line; however, the family only accepted individuals who, tracing their lineage back through generations, could demonstrate the successive steps of their descent from a common ancestor. In contrast, the clan (gens) also included those who claimed descent from a common ancestor but were unable to fully establish the intermediate links, thereby determining the degree of their relationship with one another. This term denoted not the guests, that is, the members of other similar circles who were temporarily residing in another household than their own. It also did not apply to slaves, who were viewed as property under the law and not as members of the household. Instead, it referred to individuals who lived within a protected commonwealth, even if they were not free citizens of that state. The position of protected dependents was naturally altered so far that freedmen and clients of every patron received toleration in the community at large; indeed, they were immediately dependent on the protection of the family to which they belonged. However, the very nature of the case implied that clients of members of the community could not be wholly excluded from its worship and festivals, although they were not capable of enjoying the proper rights or fulfilling the proper duties of burgesses.

The king as the head of the family was the central figure, and the clans that rested on a familial basis were the fundamental building blocks of the state. The structure of the state was thus modeled after the family, both in its overall organization and in its detailed workings. The agreements he concluded on behalf of the community with foreigners were binding on the entire population. However, in cases where a member of the community entered into an agreement with someone outside the community, that individual was not bound by the agreement. A formal cooperation in the election of a king pertained to the burgesses only after his nomination. The de jure basis of the kingly office was based on the permanent college of the Fathers (-patres-), which, through the interim holder of the power, installed the new king for life.

The unity of the Roman people was represented by two figures: the Roman Diovis in the field of religion and the prince in the field of law. As a result, their costumes were identical, reflecting the supreme god's attire. Even in the city, where everyone else walked on foot, the Roman king rode a chariot adorned with an ivory sceptre featuring an eagle. His face was painted vermilion, and he wore a golden chaplet made from oaken leaves. It would be a great mistake, however, to regard the Roman constitution on that account as a theocracy. The Romans had distinct concepts of god and king, which remained separate and distinct from one another, unlike in Egypt and the East where these ideas blended together. The Roman constitutional tradition deserves credit for its role in shaping the burgesses' divisions. Historically, this tradition accounts for the other divisions within the city's burgesses. The division into curies is believed to have originated with the city's founding. This view is consistent with the curial constitution, which is also present in Rome. Furthermore, a recently discovered scheme of Latin community organization reveals that the curial system is an essential component of the Latin municipal system.

We may not therefore conceive the number of the clans and households as legally fixed; if the curiahad to furnish a hundred men on foot and ten horsemen, it is not affirmed by tradition or credible that one horseman was taken from each clan and one foot soldier from each household. This organization could not have been introduced primarily with a view to voting, as it would have resulted in an uneven number of subdivisions. In connection with this, it's worth recalling that in Italy, there is no recorded evidence of an indigenous population less capable of culture than those who arrived later as Latin immigrants. They had no conquered race to deal with, so there was no need for a social hierarchy like the one that led to the Indian system of caste. This was in contrast to the nobility of Thessaly and Sparta, as well as perhaps the rest of Hellas, and likely also influenced the Germanic distinction of ranks.

The burgesses were both the "body of warriors" (-populus-, related to -populari-, to lay waste) and the spear-armed body of warriors (-pilumnus poplus-). In ancient litanies, it was upon this body that the blessing of Mars is invoked. Even the designation with which the king addresses them, as Quirites, signifies "warrior". "We have already stated how the army of aggression, known as the 'gathering' (legio), was formed." In cases of need, a contribution (-tributum-) was imposed, which was viewed as a forced loan and repaid when times improved. It is unclear whether this burden fell on the burgesses generally or only on the metoeci; the latter assumption is more plausible. The property of the state was not identical to the private property of the king. Judging from the statements about the extensive landed possessions of the last Roman royal house, the Tarquins, this property must have been considerable.

The rights of the burgesses extended beyond mere contribution and service. They played a significant role in the public government. The burgesses, however, were always summoned not to speak, but to listen. As the legal relation between ruler and ruled was itself sanctioned after the manner of a contract, the sovereign acts of the community were accomplished through oral questions and answers. The king would pose a question (-rogatio-) to the burgesses, and the majority of the curies would respond with an affirmative answer.

Law among the Romans was not primarily conceived as a command addressed by the sovereign to the entire community. Instead, it was viewed as a contract between the constituent powers of the state, established through exchange and response. Such a legislative contract was de jure prerequisite in all cases involving a deviation from the ordinary consistency of the legal system. The freeman could not lose or surrender his inalienable right to freedom. Therefore, someone who was not under the authority of a housemaster could not voluntarily submit to another authority figure, such as a guardian, unless the community granted him permission to do so. It was necessary to consult them when an aggressive war was contemplated, but not during defensive wars, where the other state had broken the treaty or at the conclusion of peace. However, it seems that the question was directed not to the usual assembly of burgesses, but rather to the army.

The assembly of the community, though initially restricted and limited in its early stages, had been an integral part of the Roman commonwealth since ancient times. It held a position of authority that surpassed mere coordination with the monarch. To express the matter briefly, the position of the senate as something other and more than a mere state-council—than an assemblage of trusted men whose advice the king found fitting to obtain—depended entirely on the fact that it was once an assembly, like that described by Homer, of princes and rulers of the people sitting in deliberation around the king. The selection of senators certainly lay with the king after there were no longer heads of clans. However, during this earlier epoch, as long as the people retained a vivid sense of individual clan identity, it was likely that when a senator died, the king would call another experienced and aged man from the same clanship to fill his place.

The Interregnum

The prerogatives of this council of elders were based on the view that the rule over the community composed of clans rightfully belonged to the collective clan-elders. However, in accordance with the monarchical principle of the Romans, which already found a stern expression in the household, the rule could only be exercised for the time being by one of these elders, namely the king. Every member of the senate acted as such, not in practice but in prerogative, like the king of the community. Consequently, their insignia, although inferior to those of the king, shared a similar character. The senator's insignia resembled the king's, with one notable exception: the king's was higher and more ornate than that of the senator. The interrex was entitled and bound not merely to perform all official acts pertaining to the king, but even to nominate a king for life—with the single exception that this latter right was not vested in the first who held the office. This was presumably because the first was regarded as defectively appointed, as he was not nominated by his predecessor.

If therefore the senate seemed to the Greeks to be an assembly of kings, this was only what was to be expected; for it had been so from its inception. The senate, in its collective capacity, was never consulted in the leadership of the army or in the administration of justice. As a result, there was no rightful authority for military command and no jurisdiction vested in the Roman Senate. The Senate as a state council was likely connected to the ancient usage, where the king would submit proposals to the senate, who in turn would provide their opinions to the burgesses. It was from this usage of giving counsel that the subsequent extensive powers of the senate were developed. This development was more significant than the prerogatives previously described. The senate's original power was insignificant and consisted mainly of the ability to respond to questions posed by others.

The advice, accordingly, was not a command. The king could choose to comply with it, while the senate relied on another means: the right of cassation, which was not universally applicable. The king is regarded as the possessor and vehicle of the state's plenary power, and accordingly, acts of grace, such as pardons or reprieves, are granted solely by him. The administration of the state, on the other hand, belongs to the representatives of the people and is carried out by the executive branch, which is responsible to them. In the Roman constitution, the community of the people exercised many functions similar to those of the king in England. The right of pardon, which in England is a royal prerogative, was in Rome a communal privilege. All government authority was vested in the president of the state.

These were the principles on which the community of Rome governed itself—a free people, understanding the duty of obedience and clearly disowning all mystical priestly delusion. They were absolutely equal in the eye of the law and one with another, bearing the sharply defined impress of a nationality of their own. At the same time, they wisely as well as magnanimously opened their gates wide for intercourse with other lands. This was not merely the case under the old religious marriage; the civil marriage also, although not in itself giving proprietary power over his wife to the husband, opened up the way for him to acquire this power. The legal concepts of "formal delivery" and "prescription" were applied without ceremony to such a marriage. He had not yet acquired it, and this is particularly true during the time that passed before the completion of the prescription. The wife was not considered his wife (-not uxor-) but rather his wife's representative (-pro uxore-).

The following epitaph, although belonging to a much later period, is not unworthy of being included here. It is self-evident that Roman citizenship was a condition for both royal office and consulate, making it unnecessary to explicitly deny the fictions surrounding the burgess of Cures. The term "part" (tribus) refers to what has once been or may become a whole, thereby having no inherent existence in the present.

In referring to the locality, "Quirites" was never used; instead, "Rome" and "Romans" were always employed (-urbs Roma-, -populus-, -civis-, -ager Romanus-). This is because the term "Quirites" had little local significance, similar to that of "civis" or "miles". For the same reason these designations could not be combined; they did not say "civis quiris," because both denoted, though from different points of view, the same legal concept. Among the eight ritual institutions of Numa, Dionysius II. After naming the Curiones and Flamines, it is specified that the third were the leaders of the horsemen (—oi eisagmenon—).

The only statements extant regarding the tribuni celerum —the last mentioned not only proceeds from late and quite untrustworthy authorities, but is inconsistent with the meaning of the term, which can only signify "divisional leaders of horsemen," and above all the master of the horse of the republican period, who was nominated only on extraordinary occasions and no longer nominated in later times, cannot possibly have been identical with the magistracy required for the annual festival of March 19th. Laying aside the account of Pomponius, which has arisen solely out of the anecdote of Brutus dressed in increasing ignorance as history, we reach a simple result. The tribuni celeriter correspond in number and character to the tribuni militum, and they were leaders of division among the horsemen, thus being quite distinct from the magister equitum. 11  However, it also carries the connotation of a contract where the proposer sets the terms, and the other party either accepts or declines. This was typically the case. g

The Roman community had two guilds: the Salii and the Luperci. It also had two forms of Mars, which led to two priests serving that deity. The Palatine priest would later be known as the priest of Mars, while the Colline priest was referred to as the priest of Quirinus. It is likely, although it can no longer be proved, that all the old Latin priesthoods of Rome—the Augurs, Pontifices, Vestals, and Fetials—originated in the same way from a combination of the priestly colleges of the Palatine and Quirinal communities. In the case of synoikismos, the annexed community was recognized after union as at least a tribe within the new burgess-body. This gave it some sense of political existence. However, this approach was not adopted in the case of the Hill-Romans or any later processes of annexation.

After the union, the Roman community was to be divided as formerly into three tribes, each containing ten curiae; and the Hill-Romans, whether they were or were not previously distributed into tribes of their own, must have been inserted into the existing tribes and curiae. This insertion was probably so arranged that each tribe and wardship received its assigned proportion of the new burgesses. However, the new burgesses in these divisions were not amalgamated completely with the old. Instead, the tribes henceforth presented two ranks: the Tities, Ramnes, and Luceres, which were subdivided into first and second ranks.

The Roman cavalry was raised to six units after the union, with each unit consisting of 100 horsemen. This arrangement is especially apparent in the army. If we have rightly assumed that the contrast between the Palatine old and the Quirinal new burgesses was identical with the contrast between the first and second Tities, Ramnes, and Luceres, it was thus the gentes of the Quirinal city that formed the "second" or lesser class. "The distinction, however, was more of an honorary precedent than a legal one." It thus appears that synoikismos, as seen in the Palatine community's incorporation of the Quirinal, represented an intermediate stage between the earliest synoikismos, where the Tities, Ramnes, and Luceres merged, and subsequent instances.

The annexed community was no longer allowed to form a separate tribe in the new whole, but it was permitted to furnish at least a distinct portion of each tribe. Its ritual institutions were allowed to subsist and were even elevated into institutions of the united community, a course that was not pursued in any subsequent instance. In the first place, the community might itself possess half-free clients as well as slaves. After the conquest of a town and the breaking up of its commonwealth, it often appears advisable not to sell the mass of burgesses formally as slaves. Instead, allowing them de facto possession of freedom would be preferable. In this capacity, they could enter into relations with either the clans or the king. The clients and their posterity did not possess either the rights of burgesses or those of guests by virtue of their position. To constitute a burgess, a formal bestowal of the privilege was requisite on the part of the community. In contrast, the relation of guest presumed the holding of burgess-rights in a community that had a treaty with Rome.

They obtained a legally protected possession of freedom. The body of metoeci outgrew these fetters as they began to acquire and alienate in their own name, claiming and obtaining legal redress from the Roman burgess-tribunals without the need for their patron's formal intervention. In matters of marriage and inheritance, equality of rights with the burgesses was far sooner conceded to foreigners than to those who were strictly non-free and belonged to no community. However, the latter could not be well prohibited from contracting marriages in their own circle and forming legal relations arising out of marriage—those of marital power, agnatio, and gentilitas of heritage, as well as tutelage—after the model of corresponding relations among the burgesses.

It allowed on one hand that every man capable of making a disposition had the entirely unlimited disposal of his property during his lifetime. On the other hand, so far as we know, it granted to everyone who was entitled to have dealings with Roman burgesses, even foreigners and clients, the unlimited right to acquire movable property, as well as within certain limits, immovable estate in Rome. In ancient Rome, the burgesses and non-burgesses formed an uneasy alliance. Initially, the burgesses acted as protectors, while the non-burgesses were protected by them. However, as time passed, it became increasingly challenging to reconcile this arrangement with the actual state of affairs in Rome, which was similar to other communities that allowed settlement but did not grant full citizenship rights. The burdens of war fell exclusively on the old burgesses, who were constantly thinning the ranks of their patrician descendants. The metoeci, however, shared in the results of victory without having to pay for it with their blood.

The fact that it has continued for a prolonged period is scarcely comprehensible by a numerous community, given the bestowal of Roman burgess-rights on several distinguished foreign clans. These grants appear to have occurred but sparingly from the start, and their frequency decreased as the franchise's value increased. A likely cause of greater influence was the introduction of civil marriage, whereby a child born to patrician parents who lived together as married couples, but without the requirement of -confarreatio-, acquired equal burgess rights like the child of a -confarreatio- marriage. In earlier times, clients relied solely on their patrons for legal protection. As the state solidified and the significance of clanships and households decreased, individuals had to seek justice and compensation for injuries more frequently, even without their patron's involvement, and turned to the king for assistance.

Many non-burgesses, especially those from the dissolved Latin communities, likely never had a place as clients of the royal or powerful clans. Instead, they followed the king's rule almost identically to how the burgesses did. The Servian Constitution

The first step towards the union of the two peoples did not occur until the revolutionary period, when their opposition seemed to foretell its arrival. As the land was apportioned at that time, nearly half of the farms were full hides. Each class possessed three-fourths, half, and a quarter of a hide, totaling only about one-eighth of the freeholders. Those who held an eighth of a hide made up almost exactly one-eighth of the total.

The number of divisions was tripled. The only difference in this case was that the six existing divisions with old names (-Tities-, -Ramnes-, -Luceres- and -primiand- and -secundi-) were left to the patricians, while the twelve new divisions were formed chiefly from non-burgesses. The male population capable of bearing arms was divided into two levies: the first, comprising those who had reached their 18th year and were still serving, were primarily used for field service. The second levy consisted of those who had completed their 46th year and served as garrisons at home. This phalanx was the army destined for the field; a similar force of troops was counted among those who remained behind to defend the city.

To those who were to be added, the cavalry consisted of 1,800 horses. Often, when the army took the field, however, only one-third of the total number was attached to it. As the population grew, the number of centuries remained unchanged, but the various divisions were reinforced with additional personnel, while still maintaining a focus on their core principles. Although the organization of the centuries was introduced merely to enlarge the military resources of the burgesses by the inclusion of the metoeci, there is no greater error than to exhibit the Servian organization as the introduction of a timocracy in Rome. However, the new obligation imposed upon the inhabitants to bear arms had a material influence on their political position. Although the institution of the centuries was not intended to curtail the political privileges exclusively possessed by the burgesses, it was inevitable that those rights, which the burgesses had hitherto exercised as a levy, would pass over to the new centuries of burgesses and metoeci. By the side of these new landowning full-burgesses stood the domiciled foreigners from the allied Latium, participating in public burdens, tribute, and task-works. The burgesses not domiciled, beyond the pale of the tribes, and without the right to serve in war or vote, were only seen as owing tribute.

In this way, while hitherto there had been distinguished only two classes of members of the community, burgesses and clients, there were now established three political classes that exercised a dominant influence over the constitutional law of Rome for many centuries. For the same reason, when the levy was enlarged after the admission of the Hill-Romans, the equites were doubled, while in the infantry force, instead of a single legion being formed by a "gathering" (-legio-), two legions were called out (vi). Athens, like Rome, opened its gates to the metoeci at an early period, and later invited them to share in the state's burdens.

No Italian Homer has preserved for us a picture of these earliest frays and plundering excursions, in which the character of nations is moulded and expressed like the mind of the man in the sports and enterprises of the boy. Historical tradition, however, does not enable us to form an accurate judgment on the outward development of power and the comparative resources of the several Latin cantons. The Romans' earliest military triumphs were preserved, albeit in a legendary context. The city of Alba, an ancient sacred metropolis in Latium, was conquered and subsequently destroyed by Roman forces. We are aware that little is known about the subjugation and destruction of Alba by Rome. It is not improbable that, at the same period when Rome was establishing herself on the Anio and Alban hills, Praeneste, which later became mistress to eight neighboring townships, including Tibur and others of the Latin communities, were similarly occupied in expanding their territory and laying the foundations for their subsequent considerable power.

Even the temples often remained at the same location. This is evident in the cases of Alba and Caenina, two towns that likely continued to hold some significance for religion even after their destruction. The conquered, however, were not unfrequently compelled either as a whole or in part to settle in their new capital. This is proved more satisfactorily than all the stories from the legendary period of Latium could prove it. The Roman state-law maxim states that only he who had extended the territory's boundaries was entitled to advance the city wall (the pomerium). In the time of the Roman Empire, recognized Alban clans persisted among the burgesses of Rome after the fall of their native seat. Among these were the Julii, Servilii, Quinctilii, Cloelii, Geganii, Curiatii, and Metili. The memory of their descent was preserved by their Alban family shrines, including the sanctuary of the Julii at Bovillae, which again rose to great repute under the empire.

Rome adhered to the principle of unity with greater consistency, earnestness, and success than any other Italian canton. Just as Athens' prominent position in Hellas was the result of her early centralization, Rome's greatness was solely due to the same system, which was applied more energetically in her case. The Hegemony of Rome over Latium—Alba had a special significance beyond its territorial and population extensions. The conquests of Rome in Latium were largely direct extensions of her territory and people, but the conquest of Alba held a further importance. The Roman dominance over Latium was widely acknowledged, but some communities, like Labici and especially Gabii, initially resisted its authority. Although intermarriage between different communities was not uncommon in the league, it is worth noting that such unions were often a result of frequent interactions between the various groups.

In ancient Rome, Latin citizens could exercise political rights only if they were enrolled as a burgess. However, there was also an implied equality of private rights, allowing any Latin to take up residence in any place within the Latin territory. This general right of settlement was co-extensive with the confederacy and existed alongside the special burgess-rights of individual communities. Once a plebeian was recognized as a burgess in Rome, this right became equivalent to full freedom of settlement. It is easy to understand how this should have turned to the advantage of the capital, which alone in Latium offered the means for urban intercourse, acquisition, and enjoyments. The number of metoeci in Rome should have increased remarkably after the Latin land came into a state of perpetual peace with Rome. In fact, Alba exercised her right to preside over the federal council, while Rome allowed its Latin deputies to hold their consultations independently under the guidance of a president chosen from among themselves. Alba contented herself with an honorary presidency at the federal festival, where sacrifices were offered for both Rome and Latium, and also erected a second federal sanctuary in Rome—the temple of Diana on the Aventine—so that from then on, sacrifices were offered on Roman soil for Rome and Latium, as well as on Latin soil for Latium and Rome.

The supreme command lay with the Roman generals. Year by year, the Latin contingent appeared before the gates of Rome, saluting their elected commander by acclamation as general. The Romans, commissioned by the Latin federal council to take auspices, thus assured themselves of the gods' contentment with the chosen leader. Extension of the Roman Territory after the Fall of Alba: Hernici, Rutulli, and Volscii

After the fall of Alba, Rome expanded its territory to include the Hernici, Rutulli, and Volscii. With this expansion, Rome became mistress of a considerable territory and likely the leading power in the Latin confederacy. As regards the Sabines and Aequi, Rome appears in a more advantageous position. The connection that later became so intimate with the more distant Hernici must have had at least its beginning under the monarchy. The united Latins and Hernici then enclosed Rome on two sides and checked their eastern neighbors.

The extent of the territory reduced under Roman rule at the end of the monarchy cannot be precisely determined. The regal period laid not only the political foundations of Rome but also the foundations of her external power. It cannot be doubted that this was a crucial aspect of Rome's rise to prominence. The city's position as a distinct entity, separate from the league of Latin states, is already evident at the beginning of the republic. This distinction enables us to infer that an energetic development of external power must have occurred in Rome during the time of the kings. Enlargement of the City of Rome—Servian Wall

As the Latin stock unified under Rome's leadership, it was also expanding its territory on both the east and south. Meanwhile, Rome itself underwent significant transformation, thanks to fortune's favour and the energy of its citizens. It evolved from a bustling commercial and rural town into the powerful capital of a thriving country.

The amalgamation of the adjoining communities on the Quirinal and Palatine must have been already accomplished when the Servian reform took place. Following this reform, the community united and consolidated its military strength, allowing the burgesses to no longer rest content with entrenching the several hills. Instead, they built buildings on each hill, effectively filling them in. The island in the Tiber was also occupied, as well as the height on the opposite bank, which enabled the community to command the course of the river. The Palatine had been the stronghold, but now this hill was left open to be built upon by the growing city. On the other hand, the Tarpeian Hill, standing free on every side and easily defensible due to its moderate extent, was constructed as a new stronghold (-arx-). It contained the spring of the stronghold, a carefully enclosed "well-house" (-tullianum-), the treasury (-aerarium-), the prison, and the most ancient place of assembly for the burgesses (-area Capitolina-). In later times, regular announcements of the changes in the moon's phases were still made at this location. The Capitol was in reality as well as in name the Acropolis of Rome, an independent fortress that could be defended even after the city had fallen. Its gate likely lay near what would later become the Forum. The Aventine appears to have been fortified in a similar style, although less strongly, and was preserved free from permanent occupation.

The work, however, was not complete as long as the ground, protected by laborious exertions from outward foes, was not also reclaimed from the dominion of the water. This permanently occupied the valley between the Palatine and the Capitol, suggesting there may have been a ferry there. The resulting marshes transformed the valleys between the Capitol and Velia, and those between the Palatine and Aventine. The subterranean drains still existing today are composed of magnificent square blocks, which excited the astonishment of posterity as a remarkable work of Roman engineering. This must rather be reckoned to belong to a later period, for travertine is the material used in such structures during the republic era; however, the scheme itself belongs beyond doubt to the regal period, likely predating the design of the Servian wall and the Capitoline stronghold. At one side of the comitium, which adjoined the stronghold, and upon the stronghold wall that rose above the comitium in the fashion of a balcony, the members of the senate and the guests of the city were assigned the place of honour on occasions of festivals and assemblies of the people. The senate-house was erected at the place of assembly itself, later to be known as the Curia Hostilia.

But upon the whole, we must be content to learn from this tradition: what is indeed evident of itself—that this second creation of Rome stood in intimate connection with the commencement of her hegemony over Latium and with the remodelling of her burgess-army. Its execution, however, was not the work of a single man or generation. The Servian military constitution is essentially of a Hellenic type. It will be subsequently demonstrated that the games of the Circus were organized in accordance with a Hellenic model. It cannot be proved, and it is highly unlikely that, with respect to these towns, any actual historical curse was placed on the land where they were built, as happened at Veii, Carthage, and Fregellae.

The circumstance that Rome claims to be in a religious and political point of view the heir-at-law of Alba may be regarded as decisive of the matter. For such a claim could not be based solely on the migration of individual clans to Rome, but rather it was based on the conquest of the town. 4 The Latin allies cannot be referred to here. Their legal position was defined by federal treaties, and the law of the Twelve Tables applied only to the law of Rome. Both names, although employed as local names (-capitolium was applied to the summit of the stronghold-hill next to the river, and arxto to that next to the Quirinal), were originally appellatives corresponding exactly to the Greek —akra— and —koruphei—. Every Latin town had its own -capitolium, as did Rome.

The gate itself had vanished beneath the massive structures erected in later ages on the Clivus. Rom As "Seven-hill-city," probably because the festival of the Septimontium, which was celebrated with great zeal even under the Empire, began to be regarded as a festival for the city generally; but there was hardly any definite agreement reached as to which of the heights embraced by the Servian ring-wall belonged to the seven hills. The seven mountains that are well-known to us, namely Cholongshan, Taibai Mountain, Wutai Mountain, Hua Shan, Song Shan, and Hebei Mountains. The city was built on the site of an ancient settlement. 206 seq The Janiculum is often overlooked when calculating the distance, simply because doing so would result in an eight.

The Etruscans likely conquered the southern regions from the Umbrians at a time later than their occupation of the northern areas near the Ciminian Forest. An Umbrian population persisted in these districts even after the Tuscan takeover. The southern portion of Etruria became Latinized more rapidly than the north due to the Roman conquest. In contrast, northern Etruria retained its unique culture and language for a longer period. The Umbrians were driven back from obstinate struggles into the narrow mountainous country between the two arms of the Apennines, a region they subsequently held. This is clearly indicated by their geographical position, much like the current situation of the Grisons and Basques inhabitants, who share a similar fate. In consequence of the pressure exerted upon them from the north, the Umbrians advanced towards the south, generally staying on high ground. They found the plains already occupied by Latin settlements, but made frequent incursions into the territory of their kindred race and intermingled with them more easily. This blending would have obscured any distinct differences in language and customs that might have existed earlier.

The chief branch, however, of the Umbrian stock threw itself eastward from Sabina into the mountains of the Abruzzi, and the adjacent hill country to the south of them. As on the west coast, they occupied the mountainous districts, whose thinly scattered population gave way to the immigrants or submitted to their rule. In the plain along the Apulian coast, the ancient native population, the Iapygians, maintained their ground, despite being involved in constant feuds, particularly on the northern frontier around Luceria and Arpi. While the Umbrians fell to an unequal struggle, the western offshoots of the same stock merged with the Latin or Hellenic population. In contrast, the Sabellian tribes thrived in their isolated mountain homeland, remaining separate from the Etruscans, Latins, and Greeks.

There was little or no development of urban life amongst them. Their geographical position almost wholly precluded them from engaging in commercial intercourse. The mountain tops and strongholds were sufficient for their defence needs, while husbandmen dwelled in open hamlets or wherever they found a well-spring and the forest or pasture they desired. The conquests, however, that the Samnites made along the coasts of the Tyrrhenian and Ionic seas, belong to a later age. During the Roman royal period, they appear to have been merely gaining control over the settlements where we later found them. The aspirate consonants were treated similarly. The Italians rejected them except for the aspirated -b and -f, while the Greeks reversed this rejection and kept all other sounds, including theta, phi, and chi. The Etruscans allowed the softest and most pleasing of these, phi, to drop entirely except in words borrowed from other languages. However, they used the other three sounds extensively, even when they didn't belong, as seen in Thetis becoming -Thethis-, Telephus turning into -Thelaphe-, and Odysseus evolving into -Utuzeor- or -Uthuze-.

Proper names are formed substantially in accordance with the general Italian system. As these analogies may have originated from the subsequent political and religious relations between the Etruscans and Latins, their validity is not undermined by these connections. Instead, they support the conclusion drawn from other observed phenomena: that Tuscan differed significantly from all Graeco-Italian dialects, just as the languages of the Celts or Slavonians did. The Etruscans differed significantly from the Graeco-Italian family of languages. However, no one has yet established a connection between them and any other known race. Even if we grant that, the Etruscan people appears to be scarcely less isolated. As Dionysius once said, "The Etruscans are like no other nation in language and manner." We can add nothing to this statement.

The emperor Tiberius had asked who Hecuba's mother was. We must therefore seek out the earlier homeland of the Etruscans to the west or north of Italy. It is a very ancient concept: it appears even in Herodotus; and it reappears in later writers with numerous changes and additions. Despite several intelligent inquirers, such as Dionysius, who emphatically declared their disbelief in it, there is no apparent similarity between the Lydians and Etruscans in religion, laws, manners, or language.

The term Tyrrhenians refers to the Lydian Torrhebi, as seen in the earliest sources, such as the Homeric hymns. It also denotes the Pelasgian nation under the form Tyrrheno-Pelasgians or simply Tyrrhenians. Additionally, it sometimes refers to the Italian Etruscans, although they never had lasting contact with the Pelasgians or Torrhebians, nor were they connected by common descent. To the south of the Po River, Etruscans and Umbrians mingled, with the former dominating the latter as an older race. The Etruscans had founded the old commercial towns of Atria and Spina, while the Tuscans are believed to have established Felsina (Bologna) and Ravenna. All the regions, however, to the north of the Apennines passed too rapidly from one nation to another, preventing the formation of any continuous national development.

Although Ligurians or Umbrians may have once settled there, their traces have been largely erased by the Etruscan occupation and civilization. We have already noticed the fact that the territory between the Ciminian range and the Tiber, which includes the towns of Sutrium, Nepete, Falerii, Veii, and Caere, was not taken possession of by the Etruscans until a period considerably later than the more northern districts. This may have occurred as late as the second century of Rome. It is likely that the original Italian population maintained its ground in this region, particularly in Falerii, despite being in a state of dependence. As for the migration of Etruscan bodies to Rome, we come across an isolated statement in Tuscan annals. It states that a group of Tuscan men, led by Caelius Vivenna of Volsinii, were transported to Rome after his death. Following his demise, Mastarna, his loyal companion, took charge and guided the group to their destination.

This may be trustworthy, although the derivation of the name "Caelian Mount" from Caelius is evidently a philological invention. The addition that Mastarna became king in Rome under the name of Servius Tullius is likely an improbable conjecture by archaeologists exploring legendary parallels. Tanaquil, a female name interwoven with legend, is found in Etruria. The history of the Tarquins was centered in Latium, not Etruria. During the entire royal period, Etruria had no significant impact on either the Roman language or customs, nor did it disrupt the regular development of the Roman state or the Latin League. The cause of Etruria's comparatively passive attitude towards its neighbouring land of Latium is likely due to two main factors. Firstly, the struggles of the Etruscans with the Celts on the Po River may have contributed to this passivity. It is probable that the Celts did not cross into Italy until after the expulsion of the kings from Rome. Secondly, the Etruscan people's natural inclination towards seafaring and maritime supremacy likely played a significant role in their attitude. This tendency is evident in their settlements in Campania, which we will explore further in the next chapter.

Kings or Lucumones ruled, possessing similar insignia and likely a comparable amount of power as the Roman kings. Each of these leagues consisted of twelve communities, which recognized a metropolis, especially for purposes of worship, and a federal head or rather a high priest. They appeared to be substantially equal in respect of rights; some at least were so powerful that neither could establish a hegemony nor could the central authority attain consolidation. It was, however, quite unusual for the Etruscans to act in concert, just as it was for the Latin confederacy.

As the Italian peninsula basked in the morning's early light, the eastern Mediterranean basin had already given rise to a thriving civilization. The circumstances of its geographical position, however, prevented this influence from being brought to bear upon the peninsula. The Phoenicians were a civilization that flourished along the Mediterranean coast, but they did not have a presence in Italy. In fact, the Phoenicians were a seafaring people who established colonies and trading posts throughout the Mediterranean, including those in North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean. They had no influence on Italy because it was already inhabited by other civilizations, such as the Etruscans and the Greeks, when they arrived. All the oldest relations, indeed, of the Italians with the civilization of the east point decidedly towards Greece. The rise of the Phoenician factory at Caere can be explained without resorting to the pre-Hellenic period by the subsequent well-known relations between the commercial state of Caere and Carthage.

There is no historical evidence to support the claim that the Phoenicians had a direct influence on the Italians. Greeks in Italy—Home of the Greek Immigrants. To all appearance, the Hellenic mariners were the first among the inhabitants of the eastern basin of the Mediterranean to navigate the coasts of Italy. The origin of the Greek seafarers is a matter of debate, but their arrival in the region can be pinpointed with relative accuracy.

After the discovery of America, European nations competed to send expeditions and establish settlements. The new settlers, once among barbarians, realized that they shared a common character and interests with other civilized Europeans more strongly than they had at home. The original Ionian group, encompassed under the name of the Chalcidian towns, included in Italy Cumae with other Greek settlements at Vesuvius and Rhegium, and in Sicily Zankle (later Messana), Naxos, Catana, Leontini, and Himera. The Dorian group encompassed Syracuse, Gela, Agrigentum, and most of the Sicilian colonies. In Italy, only Taras (Tarentum) and its nearby Heraclea were under its control.

The Greeks, not lonians, were a trading and sea-faring people. It was only at a comparatively late period that the Dorians descended from their inland mountains to the seaboard, and they maintained a distance from maritime commerce. We can still distinctly recognize a certain order of sequence. It is assumed that the Greeks first encountered the Italians in Cumae. This assumption makes it straightforward to understand why the Greeks used the name "Opicans," which referred to a group settled around Cumae, to describe the Italians collectively for centuries.

Naxos in Sicily was said to be the oldest of all the Greek towns founded through strict colonization. The Achaean and Dorian colonizations followed, but only after a later period. It appears that fixing the dates of this series of events is highly unlikely to achieve even approximate accuracy. The Achaean Greeks ruled a vast territory, from the coast to the interior, in the regions known as Oinotria (the land of wine) and Italia (the land of oxen), or the great Hellas. The native agricultural population was forced to farm their lands and pay tribute to them as clients or serfs.

For the Italian Achaeans, skilled in great independence, struck large, thin silver coins from two similar dies, partly cut in relief and partly sunk. These coins featured inscriptions and displayed the advanced organization of a civilized state through the mode of impression, which carefully protected them from counterfeiting common in that age—the practice of plating inferior metal with thin silver foil. None of the brilliant names in Greek art or literature shed glory on the Italian Achaean people, but Sicily could claim many of them. Even in Italy, the city of Rhegium produced an Ibycus, while Tarentum gave birth to Archytas. The Achaeans' settlement in Italy had little impact on its civilization compared to other Greek colonies.

Within their own domains, they enslaved the native population and stifled the seeds of their national development.
