As Italians, they refused to adopt Greek culture through complete assimilation, thereby hindering their progress. This event, however, belongs to a later time. They by no means despised agriculture and the acquisition of territory. It was not the wont of the Hellenes, at least when they had reached their full vigor, to rest content after the manner of the Phoenicians with a fortified factory in the midst of a barbarian land.

At this time, when Tarentum was still competing with Sybaris for first place among the Greek cities of Lower Italy, its extensive commercial connections must already have been forming. However, the Tarentines seem never to have steadily and successfully directed their efforts towards a substantial extension of their territory in the same way as the Achaean cities did. Greek cities near Vesuvius. The easternmost Greek settlements in Italy flourished rapidly, while those farther north, in the vicinity of Vesuvius, achieved a more modest prosperity. The Cumaeans crossed from the fertile island of Aenaria (Ischia) to the mainland and built a second home on a hill near the sea. From this location, they founded the seaport of Dicaearchia (later known as Puteoli), as well as the "new city" Neapolis.

Relations of the Adriatic Regions to the Greeks:

On one side of the straits of Rhegium, the southern coast of the mainland and its western coast as far as Vesuvius were Greek territory. On the other side, the larger eastern half of the island of Sicily was also Greek territory. In contrast, the west coast of Italy northward of Vesuvius and the entire east coast were in a distinct position. Two considerable mercantile towns, Epidamnus or Dyrrachium (now Durazzo), and Apollonia (near Avlona), were founded on the coast nearest to Greece during the Roman regal period; no old Greek colony can be identified further north, with the exception of a small settlement at Black Corcyra (Curzola). ) Nature herself appeared to direct the Hellenes thither. From the earliest times, there existed a regular traffic to that region from Corinth and even more so from the settlement at Corcyra (Corfu), founded not long after Rome (about 44). This traffic had its emporia on the Italian coast in the towns of Spina and Atria, situated at the mouth of the Po.

The Hellenism of Apulia was not established until a later period. Other traces of these very ancient voyages can be found in the Greek name of the island Aethalia (Ilva, Elba), which appears to have been one of the places earliest occupied by Greeks. This is also suggested by the seaport Telamon in Etruria, and further by two townships on the Caerite coast, Pyrgi, near Sybaris. Caere's location was particularly noteworthy. The Caerites, according to Strabo, were highly regarded by the Hellenes for their courage and moral integrity. Despite their considerable power, they refrained from engaging in robbery. It is not piracy that is thus referred to. For in this case, the merchant of Caere must have indulged in like manner as every other.

The Etruscans, however, did not limit themselves to mere robbery and pillage. The peaceful relations they had with Greek towns are confirmed by the gold and silver coins that, starting from around 200 BC, were minted by the Etruscan cities, particularly Populonia, following a Greek model and standard. Inhabiting the entire country, they controlled the great Italian free ports on both western and eastern seas. The Po's mouth and Venice were under their command on the eastern sea, while a land route from ancient times led from Pisa to Spina along the Adriatic. In southern Italy, they dominated the fertile plains of Capua and Nola.

Rivalry between the Phoenicians and Hellenes existed in Italy, where the Etruscans and Latins opposed the Hellenes to a lesser extent. This antagonism had some impact on the rivalry that dominated the Mediterranean commerce and navigation above all—the rivalry between the Phoenicians and Hellenes. During the Roman Empire's reign, two powerful nations - Greece and Asia Minor - clashed over dominance along the Mediterranean's shores, including Greece itself and the islands of Crete and Cyprus. Their struggle extended to the African, Spanish, and Celtic coastlines. The earlier Phoenician settlements, such as those in Sicily, were mercantile centers. Carthage dominated extensive territories, subjugating numerous subjects and establishing powerful fortresses.

When the Phocaeans settled around 217 BC in Alalia (Aleria), Corsica, opposite Caere, a combined fleet of Etruscans and Carthaginians appeared to expel them. The fleet numbered around 120 ships. In the ensuing naval battle—one of the earliest recorded in history—the Phocaean fleet, which was half as strong, claimed victory. However, the Carthaginians and Etruscans achieved their objective in the attack. The Phocaeans abandoned Corsica and preferred to settle at Hyde (Velia) on Lucania's less exposed coast. The Romans did not take part in these conflicts against the Greeks. Instead, friendly relations existed between the Romans and Phocaeans in Velia as well as Massilia from ancient times. The Ardeates even collaborated with the Zacynthians to establish a colony in Spain, which later became Saguntum. The Latins did not align themselves with the Hellenes to the same extent. Their neutral stance in this regard is evident from the close ties between Caere and Rome, as well as the remnants of ancient interactions between the Latins and the Carthaginians.

The Hellenes no longer gained ground. After establishing Agrigentum, they failed to acquire significant territorial gains along the Adriatic coast or on the western sea. They were also barred from accessing the Spanish waters and the Atlantic Ocean. In the Eoiai of Hesiod, it appears as the older collective name for the nation. However, it is evident that this term has been deliberately set aside and subordinated to that of Hellenes. The Italians had been familiar with the Greeks long before this period. As a result, the name "Hellas" continued to be used by them as a collective term for the Greek nation, even after the Greeks themselves began using alternative forms of self-identification.

The three ancient Oriental forms of the —"id:i" (—"id:S"), —"id:l" (—"id:/"), and —"id:r" (—"id:P") were often confused with those of the —"id:s", —"id:g", and —"id:p". To address this, early signs such as —"id:I" (—"id:L"), —"id:R" were proposed for substitution. These signs remained in exclusive use among Achaean colonies, while other Greeks in Italy and Sicily adopted them either exclusively or primarily, favoring the more recent forms. 3 Among Greek writers, this Tyrrhene legend of Odysseus makes its earliest appearance in Hesiod's Theogony, in one of its more recent sections. It is then found in authors of the period shortly before Alexander, including Ephorus, from whom the so-called Scymnus drew his materials, and the writer known as Scylax. The first of these sources dates back to an age when Italy was still regarded by the Greeks as a group of islands. It is certainly very old, and therefore its origin can be confidently placed in the regal period of Rome. 5

The thoughts and imaginings of the individual, however strongly they may reflect the characteristics of the national mind, are not considered part of history. The power over sons and grandsons who were under the authority of the pater familia subsisted concurrently with the royal jurisdiction. However, this power was not a true jurisdiction in the proper sense of the term, but rather a consequence of the father's inherent right to property in his children. It was applicable in cases of public treason or communion with a public enemy (proditio) and violent rebellion against the magistracy (perduellio).

But the public peace was also shattered by a foul murderer, a sodomite, one who defiled a maiden or matron's chastity, an incendiary, a false witness, those who, with evil spells, cursed the harvest, and those who, without rightful claim, cut down corn at night in fields protected by the gods and people. All these were treated as if they had committed high treason. The king opened and conducted the proceedings, then pronounced his verdict after consulting with the senators whom he had summoned to advise him. He was at liberty, however, once the process had been initiated, to delegate further handling and adjudication of the matter to deputies, typically drawn from the senate.

The later extraordinary deputies, including the two men for adjudicating on rebellion (duoviri perduellionis) and the later standing deputies known as "trackers of murder" (quaestores parricidii), whose primary duty was to search out and arrest murderers, did not belong to the regal period. Instead, they may have originated from or been influenced by certain institutions within that period. Prisoners were held during the investigation, but those awaiting trial could be released on bail. When both parties had appeared, the plaintiff stated his demand orally. The defendant similarly refused to comply. In this case, the king could either investigate the matter personally or appoint a deputy to handle it on his behalf.

Theft: What Circumstances Make it Justifiable? During this epoch, theft was considered justifiable under certain circumstances. However, the specific conditions that made theft excusable and the rights of the person injured by the theft are unclear. The injured party sought greater compensation from the thief who was caught in the act than from one discovered later. This was because the sense of frustration that needed to be alleviated was stronger in the first instance than in the second. It was not the right of the stronger that was regarded as the foundation of a title to it. On the contrary, all property was considered as conferred by the community upon the individual burgess for his exclusive possession and use. It was only the burgess, and those equal to him in this respect, who were capable of holding property.

It was not within the father's power to arbitrarily deny his children their right of inheritance, as he could neither dissolve the paternal power nor execute a will without the consent of the entire community. This consent was often refused, especially in situations like this one. The regulation that placed an individual who had alienated their hereditary property and deprived their children of it under guardianship akin to a lunatic was likely as old as the time when arable land was first divided, thus leading to private property gaining greater significance for the commonwealth.
